sources.citation: Dunlap, R. E., & Jones, R. E. (2002). Environmental concern: Conceptual and measurement issues. In R. E. Dunlap & W. Michelson (Eds.), Handbook of environmental sociology (pp. 482–524). Greenwood Press.
sources.relevance: Foundational work on conceptualizing and measuring environmental concern at multiple levels (global, national, local, personal), directly relevant to the multi-level structure of this construct.
sources.key_finding: Environmental concern is a multidimensional construct and respondents often differentiate between the severity of environmental problems at national versus local scales.

sources.citation: Lezama, J. L. (2004). La construcción social y política del medio ambiente. El Colegio de México.
sources.relevance: Analyzes how environmental problems are socially constructed in Mexico, including the salience of specific issues like water pollution, deforestation, and air quality in public discourse.
sources.key_finding: Environmental problem salience in Mexico is shaped by media framing, urbanization patterns, and proximity to industrial pollution, with significant regional variation.

sources.citation: Inglehart, R. (1995). Public support for environmental protection: Objective problems and subjective values in 43 societies. PS: Political Science and Politics, 28(1), 57–72.
sources.relevance: Examines how objective environmental conditions and subjective postmaterialist values jointly determine the salience of environmental problems across countries, including Mexico.
sources.key_finding: Both objective environmental degradation and subjective value orientations independently predict environmental concern, with developing nations showing concern driven more by direct exposure to pollution.

sources.citation: Pew Research Center. (2015). Global concern about climate change, broad support for limiting emissions. Pew Research Center.
sources.relevance: Cross-national survey data showing Mexican respondents' high levels of concern about climate change and environmental degradation relative to other countries.
sources.key_finding: Mexico ranked among the top countries in perceived seriousness of climate change, with over 70% of respondents viewing it as a very serious problem.

causal_relationships.related_construct: support for environmental governance and regulation

sources.citation: Bamberg, S., & Möser, G. (2007). Twenty years after Hines, Hungerford, and Tomera: A new meta-analysis of psycho-social determinants of pro-environmental behaviour. Journal of Environmental Psychology, 27(1), 14–25.
sources.relevance: Meta-analysis showing that environmental knowledge and perceived behavioral control (self-efficacy) are significant predictors of pro-environmental behavior, validating the combination of knowledge and self-efficacy in one construct.
sources.key_finding: Problem awareness and internal locus of control (self-efficacy) were among the strongest psycho-social predictors of pro-environmental behavior, with knowledge serving as an indirect predictor.

sources.citation: Tabernero, C., & Hernández, B. (2011). Self-efficacy and intrinsic motivation guiding environmental behavior. Environment and Behavior, 43(5), 658–675.
sources.relevance: Directly examines the role of environmental self-efficacy in motivating sustainable behavior, supporting the theoretical linkage between perceived competence and environmental engagement.
sources.key_finding: Higher environmental self-efficacy predicted greater engagement in recycling and energy conservation behaviors, mediated by intrinsic motivation.

sources.citation: Tobler, C., Visschers, V. H. M., & Siegrist, M. (2012). Consumers' knowledge about climate change. Climatic Change, 114(2), 189–209.
sources.relevance: Investigates public knowledge and misconceptions about climate change causes (including the common confusion between ozone depletion and climate change), directly relevant to items p27_1 and p36_1.
sources.key_finding: A substantial proportion of respondents conflated ozone depletion with climate change, and higher factual knowledge was associated with greater willingness to adopt mitigation behaviors.

sources.citation: González-Gaudiano, E., & Maldonado-González, A. L. (2017). Climate change perception and literacy among university students in Mexico. Ambiente & Sociedade, 20(1), 181–198.
sources.relevance: Examines environmental and climate literacy among Mexican populations, providing context for self-assessed knowledge levels and common misconceptions in the Mexican context.
sources.key_finding: Mexican university students showed moderate climate literacy but significant misconceptions about causes and mechanisms of climate change, with self-assessed knowledge often exceeding actual knowledge.

sources.citation: Stern, P. C., Dietz, T., Abel, T., Guagnano, G. A., & Kalof, L. (1999). A value-belief-norm theory of support for social movements: The case of environmentalism. Human Ecology Review, 6(2), 81–97.
sources.relevance: Proposes the Value-Belief-Norm (VBN) theory explaining environmental activism (petitions, donations, protests) as driven by values, ecological worldview, awareness of consequences, and personal norms—directly mapping onto the civic actions measured here.
sources.key_finding: Personal moral norms, activated through awareness of environmental consequences and ascription of responsibility, were the strongest direct predictors of environmental activism and willingness to sacrifice.

sources.citation: Lubell, M. (2002). Environmental activism as collective action. Environment and Behavior, 34(4), 431–454.
sources.relevance: Analyzes environmental activism (protests, petitions, donations) through a collective action framework, relevant to understanding the barriers and motivations for the specific civic behaviors measured in this construct.
sources.key_finding: Perceived collective benefits of environmental action and civic duty norms significantly predicted participation in environmental activism, while free-rider concerns reduced participation.

sources.citation: Durand Ponte, V. M. (2004). Ciudadanía y cultura política: México, 1993–2001. Siglo XXI Editores.
sources.relevance: Examines civic participation patterns in Mexico, including non-electoral forms of engagement such as petitions, protests, and donations, providing context for understanding environmental civic engagement within Mexican political culture.
sources.key_finding: Civic engagement in Mexico is constrained by low interpersonal trust and weak institutional credibility, but issue-specific mobilization (including environmental causes) has grown among urban, educated populations.

sources.citation: Martínez-Alier, J. (2002). The environmentalism of the poor: A study of ecological conflicts and valuation. Edward Elgar Publishing.
sources.relevance: Documents environmental activism in Latin America, including Mexico, driven by livelihood concerns rather than postmaterialist values, relevant to understanding the social bases of environmental civic engagement.
sources.key_finding: Environmental civic engagement in developing countries often emerges from communities directly affected by resource extraction and pollution, representing an 'environmentalism of the poor' distinct from Northern environmentalism.

causal_relationships.related_construct: general political participation and civic culture

sources.citation: Dryzek, J. S. (2013). The politics of the earth: Environmental discourses (3rd ed.). Oxford University Press.
sources.relevance: Provides a comprehensive framework for understanding different environmental governance discourses (administrative rationalism, democratic pragmatism, ecological modernization) that shape public attitudes toward state versus market responsibility for environmental protection.
sources.key_finding: Public attitudes toward environmental governance reflect underlying discursive frameworks, with preferences for state regulation versus market mechanisms varying by political culture and institutional trust.

sources.citation: Lemos, M. C., & Agrawal, A. (2006). Environmental governance. Annual Review of Environment and Resources, 31, 297–325.
sources.relevance: Reviews the concept of environmental governance including the roles of state, market, and civil society actors, directly relevant to the construct's focus on responsibility attribution and regulatory preferences.
sources.key_finding: Effective environmental governance increasingly requires hybrid arrangements combining state regulation, market incentives, and community participation, and public attitudes toward these arrangements shape policy feasibility.

sources.citation: Michaud, K., Carlisle, J., & Smith, E. (2009). Nimbyism vs. environmentalism in attitudes toward energy development. Environmental Politics, 18(5), 727–750.
sources.relevance: Examines how public attitudes toward government regulation of energy and environmental resources are shaped by competing concerns about economic development and environmental protection.
sources.key_finding: Support for government environmental regulation was strongest among those with high environmental concern and low trust in corporate self-regulation.

sources.citation: Provencio, E., & Carabias, J. (1993). El enfoque del desarrollo sustentable: Una nota introductoria. In E. Provencio & J. Carabias (Eds.), El desarrollo sustentable en México. UNAM.
sources.relevance: Discusses the evolution of environmental governance attitudes in Mexico, including the tension between economic development priorities and environmental regulation in Mexican public policy.
sources.key_finding: Mexican environmental governance attitudes are shaped by the historical dominance of developmentalist ideology, with growing but contested support for stronger environmental regulation.

causal_relationships.related_construct: attitudes toward state intervention in the economy

sources.citation: Leiserowitz, A. (2006). Climate change risk perception and policy preferences: The role of affect, imagery, and values. Climatic Change, 77(1–2), 45–72.
sources.relevance: Foundational study on how risk perception of climate change is shaped by affective imagery, cultural values, and personal experience, directly relevant to the experiential and vulnerability dimensions of this construct.
sources.key_finding: Negative affective imagery and personal risk perception were stronger predictors of climate policy support than analytical assessments of probability and consequence.

sources.citation: Brody, S. D., Zahran, S., Vedlitz, A., & Grover, H. (2008). Examining the relationship between physical vulnerability and public perceptions of global climate change in the United States. Environment and Behavior, 40(1), 72–95.
sources.relevance: Examines how physical vulnerability and direct experience with climate-related events shape risk perceptions, relevant to items on personal experience (p37) and willingness to relocate (p39).
sources.key_finding: Respondents living in areas with greater physical vulnerability to climate impacts reported significantly higher risk perceptions, even after controlling for sociodemographic factors.

sources.citation: Safi, A. S., Smith, W. J., & Liu, Z. (2012). Rural Nevada and climate change: Vulnerability, beliefs, and risk perception. Risk Analysis, 32(6), 1041–1059.
sources.relevance: Investigates how socioeconomic vulnerability intersects with climate change risk perception, relevant to the construct's inclusion of beliefs about differential impacts on poor populations (p38_1).
sources.key_finding: Perceived personal vulnerability and belief in the disproportionate impacts of climate change on disadvantaged groups were significant predictors of adaptive behavior intentions.

sources.citation: Zamora Saenz, I. B., & Rosas Rodríguez, F. (2017). Percepción social del cambio climático en México. In B. Ortiz & C. Velasco (Eds.), La percepción social del cambio climático. Universidad Iberoamericana / INECC.
sources.relevance: Directly examines Mexican public perceptions of climate change risk, including experienced impacts, vulnerability beliefs, and willingness to take adaptive action.
sources.key_finding: Mexican respondents showed high awareness of climate change but significant variation in risk perception by region, socioeconomic status, and direct experience with extreme weather events.

sources.citation: van der Linden, S. (2015). The social-psychological determinants of climate change risk perceptions: Towards a comprehensive model. Journal of Environmental Psychology, 41, 112–124.
sources.relevance: Proposes an integrative model of climate change risk perception incorporating cognitive, experiential, and socio-cultural factors, providing theoretical grounding for the multi-dimensional measurement approach of this construct.
sources.key_finding: Personal experience with extreme weather, social norms, and affect were the strongest predictors of climate change risk perception, with knowledge playing a smaller but significant role.

causal_relationships.related_construct: support for climate mitigation and adaptation policies
direct experience with natural disasters and extreme weather
